meta.title: R231 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes R231 engine — M276, M278, M157, M279. Real UK costs, reliability data, and the M276 V6 that sets the standard. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes R231 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned R231 engine typically costs £2,500–£14,000 supply only, depending on the engine — the M276 V6 sits at £2,500–£6,000, while the M278 V8 is £3,500–£7,500, the M157 AMG V8 is £5,000–£9,000, and the V12 M279 is £8,000–£14,000. Fitting adds £650–£1,500 depending on the variant. Use our calculator for a personalised quote.

mainEntity.name: Which R231 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M276 V6 (SL 350/SL 400) is the most reliable choice — it's Mercedes-developed, refined, and has a strong track record when serviced properly. The M157 AMG V8 (SL 63 AMG) is also reliable, though owners should watch for wiring harness oil intrusion. The M278 V8 (SL 500) is the least reliable of the mainstream engines.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the R231?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M278 V8 (SL 500) is known for cylinder scoring and oil/coolant leaks; early M276 V6 engines (pre-2014) can suffer from timing chain tensioner issues; and the M157 AMG V8 can develop oil intrusion into the wiring harness. Engine and transmission mounts are also known to wear prematurely. Regular servicing is key.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes R231 reliable overall?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — the R231 is generally regarded as one of the most reliable SL generations, especially with the M276 V6 and M157 AMG V8 engines. The M278 V8 requires more careful maintenance. The V12 M279 is reliable but exceptionally expensive to service. All engines benefit from regular, attentive servicing.

mainEntity.name: Which R231 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M278 V8 (SL 500) — its cylinder scoring issues can lead to catastrophic engine failure, and the fix is very expensive. Some owners have found that excessively long service intervals are partly to blame. If you're considering an SL 500, have a specialist inspect the engine thoroughly and verify the service history. The M279 V12 (SL 65 AMG) is not an engine to avoid, but buyers should be aware of the astronomical running costs.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my R231 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Warning signs include excessive oil consumption and blue smoke (M278 cylinder scoring), rattling noise on cold start (M276 timing chain), check engine light with wiring harness faults (M157 oil intrusion), or excessive vibration (engine mounts). If you notice any of these, have the car inspected immediately — ignoring them can lead to a full engine failure.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes R231 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. A well-kept R231 is worth £15,000–£45,000, so a £2,800–£6,000 M276 recon is only 6–40% of its value — well within our 70% Mercedes threshold, making replacement a sensible decision. Against a higher-mileage SL 500 with the M278, the same maths is still favourable (8–50%). The R231's strong retained values make most engine replacements economically viable. For V12 cars, replacement costs often approach or exceed the vehicle's value.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes R231 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The M276 V6 can easily exceed 100,000 miles with regular servicing. The M157 AMG V8 is similarly durable with proper maintenance. The M278 V8 has a shorter lifespan if cylinder scoring develops, though careful maintenance can mitigate the risk. The M279 V12 is reliable but demands meticulous maintenance.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the R231 and the previous R230?

acceptedAnswer.text: The R231 is the sixth-generation SL, built on an all-aluminium space-frame bodyshell that's 140 kg lighter than the R230. It introduced new engines including the M276 V6, M278 V8, and M157 AMG V8. The R230 (2001–2012) used older M112, M113, M272, and M273 engines and is covered on its own generation page.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes R231 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code is on the VIN plate (visible through the windscreen), on the vehicle documentation, and stamped on the engine block. For the R231, common codes are M276 (V6 petrol), M278 (V8 petrol), M157 (AMG V8), and M279 (AMG V12). Tell us your variant and year, and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes SL R231 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the sixth-generation SL roadster — from the 3.0-litre M276 V6 to the 4.7-litre M278 V8 and the fire-breathing M157 and M279 AMG V8s. Real UK costs, honest reliability data, and the aluminium-bodied grand tourer that redefined the SL for the modern era. Backed by 880+ real UK enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED].

confidenceScore.title: Confidence Score
confidenceScore.text: * M276.954/825: 90% confidence — well‑documented M276 V6; pattern data. * M278.927: 80% confidence — known M278 issues; provisional. * M157.983: 85% confidence — well‑documented AMG V8; pattern data. * M279.981: 75% confidence — rare V12; limited long‑term data.

overview.intro: The R231 is the sixth generation of the Mercedes‑Benz SL‑Class, a two‑seat grand tourer roadster launched in March 2012 as the successor to the R230. Built on an all‑aluminium space‑frame bodyshell, it was 140 kg lighter than its predecessor and offered a 20 per cent increase in torsional rigidity. The engine range spanned from the 3.5‑litre M276 V6 to the 4.7‑litre M278 V8, with AMG variants delivering up to 630 bhp from the M279 V12. A significant facelift arrived in 2016, bringing power upgrades and refreshed styling. Production ended in 2020, succeeded by the Mercedes‑AMG R232.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes R231 (all‑aluminium space‑frame) • Predecessor: R230 (2001–2012) • Successor: R232 (2021–present) • Sibling body styles in this generation: none (SL is standalone roadster)
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The R231 SL generated 880+ real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED], concentrated on the M276 V6 and M157 AMG V8 as the most‑requested powertrains.

items.quote: Mercedes' 3.0‑litre bi‑turbo V6, upgraded to 367 bhp in the 2016 facelift, delivers effortless performance with 500 Nm of torque. It's the sweet spot in the R231 range — refined, responsive, and considerably more reliable than the V8 alternatives.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want grand touring performance without the V8's running costs or reliability concerns.

items.quote: The 4.7‑litre bi‑turbo V8 is known for cylinder scoring and coolant/oil leaks. While it delivers effortless power, these issues can be costly to address. The M278 is widely considered the most problematic engine in the R231 range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who prioritise V8 presence and are prepared for potentially expensive maintenance.

items.quote: The 6.0‑litre twin‑turbo V12 is a masterpiece of engineering but exceptionally complex and costly to maintain. Parts are expensive, labour is intensive, and specialist knowledge is required.
items.whoItsFor: collectors and enthusiasts who want flagship V12 status and can afford the premium running costs.

items.quote: The 3.5‑litre naturally aspirated V6 offers the best balance of performance (306 bhp), characterful engine note, and reliability. It's the most affordable entry point into R231 ownership.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the SL experience without the turbocharged complexity.

items.quote: The 5.5‑litre twin‑turbo V8 delivers 537–585 bhp with genuine AMG character. It's often overlooked in favour of the V12 but offers a more engaging and reliable driving experience — watch for oil intrusion in the wiring harness.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want AMG performance without the V12's reliability headaches.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Risk vs Reward — powerful but potentially expensive.

rows.commonMajorFailure: Oil intrusion in wiring harness, engine mounts
rows.ownershipVerdict: AMG Sweet Spot — reliable V8 with manageable issues.

rows.commonMajorFailure: V12 complexity, cooling system, ignition coils
rows.ownershipVerdict: Flagship Tax — exceptional but expensive to maintain.

economicsRule.title: The R231 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,800–£6,000 M276 recon against a £15,000–£45,000 R231 sits at 6–40% of the car's value — comfortably within our 70% Mercedes threshold, making replacement a sensible decision. Against a higher‑mileage SL 500 with the M278, the same maths is still favourable (8–50%). The R231's strong retained values make most engine replacements economically viable. V12 cars are a different proposition — their replacement costs often approach or exceed the vehicle's value.

keyTakeaways.question: Which R231 Costs Least To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: SL 350 (M276.954) — best balance of performance and reliability.

keyTakeaways.question: Which R231 Costs Most To Own?
keyTakeaways.answer: SL 65 AMG (M279) — V12 running costs are astronomical.

keyTakeaways.question: Which R231 Is Best Value?
keyTakeaways.answer: SL 400 (M276.825) — the sweet spot in the range.

problems.title: M278 V8 Cylinder Scoring & Oil Leaks (SL 500)
problems.rootCause: The M278 V8 is known for cylinder scoring (bore wear) and oil/coolant leaks. Regular oil changes using Mercedes‑spec 229.5 oil are critical to mitigate these issues. Camshaft position sensors can also leak oil into the wiring harness.
problems.recommendation: On SL 500 models, adhere strictly to 5,000‑mile oil change intervals and use only Mercedes‑spec 229.5 oil — an £800–£1,500 preventative repair is far cheaper than the £3,500–£7,500 replacement that follows if cylinder scoring progresses.

cta.label: Compare M278 engine prices for your R231 SL 500 →

problems.title: M276 Timing Chain Tensioner Issues (early cars)
problems.rootCause: Some pre‑2014 M276 engines suffered from timing chain tensioner issues, causing rattling on cold start, check engine lights, and fault codes. Mercedes introduced improved parts after this date.
problems.recommendation: If you hear a rattling noise on cold start from a pre‑2014 SL 350 or SL 400, have the timing chain tensioner inspected immediately — a £600–£1,200 repair is far cheaper than the £2,500–£5,000 replacement that follows if the chain fails.

cta.label: Compare M276 engine prices for your R231 V6 →

problems.title: M157 AMG Wiring Harness Oil Intrusion (SL 63 AMG)
problems.rootCause: The M157 V8 can suffer from oil intrusion into the engine wiring harness, particularly from camshaft position sensors. This can lead to electrical faults, check engine lights, and potential engine damage if not addressed.
problems.recommendation: On SL 63 AMG models, have the wiring harness and cam sensors inspected at every service — a £600–£1,200 repair is far cheaper than the £4,000–£7,000 replacement that follows if oil contamination causes a major electrical failure.

cta.label: Compare M157 engine prices for your R231 SL 63 AMG →

problems.title: Engine & Transmission Mount Failure
problems.rootCause: The R231's engine and transmission mounts can fail prematurely, leading to excessive movement and vibration. This is particularly noticeable when shifting between drive and reverse.
problems.recommendation: Have the engine and transmission mounts inspected at every service — a £300–£600 repair is a minor expense compared to the cost of more serious drivetrain issues.

cta.label: Compare R231 engine mount replacement prices →

replacementCosts.h2: R231 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page's baseline figures — the R231 is a standalone model with no direct platform‑sharing for engine bay access, so labour times are specific to this generation. Costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are R231 sixth‑generation SL only. The R231 was not available with a non‑AMG V12 — the SL 600 was discontinued. The SL 400 replaced the SL 350 from November 2014, and the facelift in 2016 brought power upgrades to both the SL 400 (367 bhp) and SL 500 (455 bhp).

mostRequestedEngines: 1. M276 (V6 petrol) — most‑requested across the Mercedes range [MB‑VERIFIED]
2. M157 (5.5 AMG V8) — strong enthusiast demand for the SL 63
3. M278 (4.7 V8) — demand for the SL 500
mostCommonFailures: 3. M157 wiring harness oil intrusion (SL 63 AMG)
